frameworks.name: neural variability quenching
frameworks.one_line: Post-stimulus reduction of trial-to-trial variability in cortical populations

frameworks.name: stimulus-induced oscillatory power suppression
frameworks.one_line: Alpha-beta band power decreases after stimulus onset

frameworks.name: cortical population oscillations
frameworks.one_line: Band-limited oscillations couple to single-trial variability structure

core_question: Are trial-by-trial variability quenching and stimulus-induced oscillatory power suppression tightly coupled in large cortical populations?
key_assumptions: MEG reflects activity of large cortical neural populations
Trial-to-trial variability and oscillatory power can be decomposed by frequency band
Across-trial standard deviation indexes neural variability
manipulations: Repeated presentations of a visual stimulus to healthy subjects
Selective exclusion of the alpha-beta band from the recordings
Selective exclusion of other frequency bands as control
key_findings: Timing, amplitude, and spatial topography of variability-quenching and power-suppression were remarkably similar
Excluding the alpha-beta band eliminated variability quenching
Excluding other frequency bands did not eliminate variability quenching
Alpha-beta band power explained 86% of between-subject differences in variability quenching
interpretations: Variability quenching and oscillatory power suppression are tightly coupled, likely reflecting a shared cortical mechanism
Alpha-beta oscillations are the principal driver of trial-by-trial neural variability in large populations
